   950	        &mut self,
   951	        compiled_case: &'a CompiledCase,
   952	        subjects: &'a [TypedExpr],
   953	    ) -> Vec<SubjectAssignment<'a>> {
   954	        let assignments = subjects
   955	            .iter()
   956	            .map(|subject| assign_subject(self.expression_generator, subject, Ordering::Strict))
   957	            .collect_vec();
   958	
   959	        for (variable, assignment) in compiled_case
   960	            .subject_variables
   961	            .iter()
   962	            .zip(assignments.iter())
   963	        {
   964	            // We need to record the fact that each subject corresponds to a
   965	            // pattern variable.
   966	            self.set_value(variable, assignment.name());
   967	            self.bind(assignment.name(), variable);
   968	        }
   969	
   970	        assignments
   971	    }
   972	
   973	    /// Give a unique name to the subject of a let expression (if it needs one
   974	    /// and it's not already a variable) and keep track of that name in case it
   975	    /// needs to be referenced later.
   976	    ///
   977	    fn assign_let_subject(
   978	        &mut self,
   979	        compiled_case: &'a CompiledCase,
   980	        subject: &'a TypedExpr,
   981	    ) -> SubjectAssignment<'a> {
   982	        let variable = compiled_case
   983	            .subject_variables
   984	            .first()
   985	            .expect("decision tree with no subjects");
   986	        let assignment = assign_subject(self.expression_generator, subject, Ordering::Loose);
   987	        self.set_value(variable, assignment.name());
   988	        self.bind(assignment.name(), variable);
   989	        assignment
   990	    }
   991	
   992	    fn local_var(&mut self, name: &EcoString) -> EcoString {
   993	        self.expression_generator.local_var(name)
   994	    }
   995	
   996	    fn next_local_var(&mut self, name: &EcoString) -> EcoString {
   997	        self.expression_generator.next_local_var(name)
   998	    }
   999	
  1000	    /// Records that a given pattern `variable` has been assigned a runtime
  1001	    /// `value`. For example if we had something like this:
  1002	    ///
  1003	    /// ```txt
  1004	    /// a is Wibble(1, b) -> todo
  1005	    /// ```
  1006	    ///
  1007	    /// After a successful `is Wibble` check, we know we'd end up with two
  1008	    /// additional checks that look like this:
  1009	    ///
  1010	    /// ```txt
  1011	    /// a0 is 1, a1 is b -> todo
  1012	    /// ```
  1013	    ///
  1014	    /// But what's the runtime value of `a0` and `a1`? To get those we'd have to
  1015	    /// extract the two fields from `a`, so they would have a value that looks
  1016	    /// like this: `a[0]` and `a[1]`; these values are set with this `set_value`
  1017	    /// function as we discover them.
  1018	    ///
  1019	    fn set_value(&mut self, variable: &Variable, value: EcoString) {
  1020	        let _ = self.variable_values.insert(variable.id, value);
  1021	    }
  1022	
  1023	    /// This is conceptually the same as set value, but it's for bit array
  1024	    /// segments instead of pattern variables.
  1025	    fn set_segment_value(
  1026	        &mut self,
  1027	        bit_array: &Variable,
  1028	        segment_name: EcoString,
  1029	        read_action: &ReadAction,
  1030	    ) {
  1031	        let value = self.read_action_to_doc(bit_array, read_action);
  1032	        let _ = self.segment_values.insert(segment_name, value);
  1033	    }
  1034	
  1035	    /// During the code generation process we might end up having to generate
  1036	    /// code to materialises one of the pattern variables and gives it a name to
  1037	    /// be used to avoid repeating it every single time.
  1038	    ///
  1039	    /// For example if a pattern variable is referencing the fifth element in a
  1040	    /// list it's runtime value would look something like this:
  1041	    /// `list.tail.tail.tail.tail.head`; if we where to perform additional
  1042	    /// checks on this value, it would be quite wasteful to recompute it every
  1043	    /// single time. Imagine this piece of code:
  1044	    ///
  1045	    /// ```gleam
  1046	    /// case list {
  1047	    ///   [_, _, _, _, 1] -> todo
  1048	    ///   [_, _, _, _, 2] -> todo
  1049	    ///   // ...
  1050	    ///   _ -> todo
  1051	    /// }
  1052	    /// ```
  1053	    ///
  1054	    /// The corresponding check would end up looking something like this:
  1055	    ///
  1056	    /// ```js
  1057	    /// if (list.tail.tail.tail.tail.head === 1) {}
  1058	    /// else if (list.tail.tail.tail.tail.head === 2) {}
  1059	    /// // ...
  1060	    /// else {}
  1061	    /// ```
  1062	    ///
  1063	    /// So before a check we might want to bind a pattern variable to a name so
  1064	    /// we can use that to reference it in the check:
  1065	    ///
  1066	    /// ```js
  1067	    /// let $ = list.tail.tail.tail.tail.head;
  1068	    /// if ($ === 1) {}
  1069	    /// else if ($ === 2) {}
  1070	    /// // ...
  1071	    /// else {}
  1072	    /// ```
  1073	    ///
  1074	    /// This makes for neater code! These bindings are kept track of with this
  1075	    /// function.
  1076	    ///
  1077	    fn bind(&mut self, name: EcoString, variable: &Variable) {
  1078	        let _ = self.scoped_variable_names.insert(variable.id, name);
  1079	    }
  1080	
  1081	    /// This has the exact same purpose as `bind` but works with bit array
  1082	    /// segments instead of pattern variables introduced during the decision
  1083	    /// tree compilation.
  1084	    ///
  1085	    fn bind_segment(&mut self, bound_to_variable: EcoString, segment: EcoString) {
  1086	        let _ = self.scoped_segment_names.insert(segment, bound_to_variable);
  1087	    }
  1088	
  1089	    fn bindings_doc(&mut self, bindings: &'a [(EcoString, BoundValue)]) -> Document<'a> {
  1090	        let bindings =
  1091	            (bindings.iter()).map(|(variable, value)| self.body_binding_doc(variable, value));
  1092	        join(bindings, line())
  1093	    }
  1094	
  1095	    fn bindings_ref_doc(&mut self, bindings: &[&'a (EcoString, BoundValue)]) -> Document<'a> {
  1096	        let bindings =
  1097	            (bindings.iter()).map(|(variable, value)| self.body_binding_doc(variable, value));
  1098	        join(bindings, line())
  1099	    }
  1100	
  1101	    fn body_binding_doc(
  1102	        &mut self,
  1103	        variable_name: &'a EcoString,
  1104	        value: &'a BoundValue,
  1105	    ) -> Document<'a> {
  1106	        let local_variable_name = self.next_local_var(variable_name);
  1107	        let assigned_value = match value {
  1108	            BoundValue::Variable(variable) => self.get_value(variable).to_doc(),
  1109	            BoundValue::LiteralString(value) => string(value),
  1110	            BoundValue::LiteralFloat(value) => float(value),
  1111	            BoundValue::LiteralInt(value) => eco_string_int(eco_format!("{value}")),
  1112	            BoundValue::BitArraySlice {
  1113	                bit_array,
  1114	                read_action,
  1115	            } => self
  1116	                .get_segment_value(variable_name)
  1117	                .unwrap_or_else(|| self.read_action_to_doc(bit_array, read_action)),
  1118	        };
  1119	
  1120	        match self.variable_assignment {
  1121	            VariableAssignment::Declare => let_doc(local_variable_name.clone(), assigned_value),
  1122	            VariableAssignment::Reassign => {
  1123	                reassignment_doc(local_variable_name.clone(), assigned_value)
  1124	            }
  1125	        }
  1126	    }
  1127	
  1128	    /// Generates the document to perform a (possibly negated) runtime check on
  1129	    /// the given variable.
  1130	    ///
  1131	    fn runtime_check(
  1132	        &mut self,
  1133	        variable: &Variable,
  1134	        runtime_check: &'a RuntimeCheck,
  1135	    ) -> Document<'a> {
  1136	        let value = self.get_value(variable);
  1137	
  1138	        let equality = " === ";
  1139	
  1140	        match runtime_check {
  1141	            RuntimeCheck::String { value: expected } => docvec![value, equality, string(expected)],
  1142	            RuntimeCheck::Float {
  1143	                float_value: expected,
  1144	            } => docvec![value, equality, float_from_value(expected.value())],
  1145	            RuntimeCheck::Int {
  1146	                int_value: expected,
  1147	            } => docvec![value, equality, expected.clone()],
  1148	            RuntimeCheck::StringPrefix { prefix, .. } => {

  1153	                // In this case we need to check that the remaining part of the
  1154	                // bit array has a whole number of bytes.

  1160	                        let remaining_bits = docvec![value, ".bitSize - ", size_so_far];

  1162	                    }

  1172	                            from: start,

  1174	                            endianness,
  1175	                            ..
  1176	                        },

  1179	                    let end = match (start.constant_bits(), size.constant_bits()) {

2026-05-31 09:38:11,334 - INFO - swea-agent-gleam-lang__gleam-5493 - 💭 THOUGHT
The `ASSIGNMENT_VAR` is `"$"` and in `expression.rs` line 227, when `name == "$"`, it uses `eco_format!("${n}")` which would produce `$1`, `$2`, etc. 

Now let me understand how the variable name tracking works. The issue is about duplicate `$1` or `$2` variable names. Let me look at the `next_local_var` and `local_var` methods more carefully:

```rust
pub fn local_var(&mut self, name: &EcoString) -> EcoString {
    match self.current_scope_vars.get(name) {
        None => {
            let _ = self.current_scope_vars.insert(name.clone(), 0);
            maybe_escape_identifier(name)
        }
        Some(0) => maybe_escape_identifier(name),
        Some(n) if name == "$" => eco_format!("${n}"),
        Some(n) => eco_format!("{name}${n}"),
    }
}

pub fn next_local_var(&mut self, name: &EcoString) -> EcoString {
    let next = self.current_scope_vars.get(name).map_or(0, |i| i + 1);
    let _ = self.current_scope_vars.insert(name.clone(), next);
    self.local_var(name)
}
```

So the variable names work like:
- First time: `$` (count = 0)
- Second time: `$1` (count = 1)
- Third time: `$2` (count = 2)

The issue from the PR description is:

```javascript
let $ = [1, 2];
let $1 = $[0];   // first use of $ - this is from a case expression subject
if ($1 === 1) {
    let b = $[1];
    _block = b;
} else {
    let b = $[1];
    _block = b;
}
let x = _block;
let $1 = [x, 3];  // ERROR: $1 already declared!
```

So the `current_scope_vars` is shared across the whole function. When we have a case expression like:
```gleam
case #(1, 2) {
  #(1, b) -> b
  #(_, b) -> b
}
```

This compiles down to something like:
```javascript
let $ = [1, 2];     // subject assignment, count 0 -> `$`
let $1 = $[0];      // Next check needs the first element, count 1 -> `$1`
if ($1 === 1) { ... }
```

Then later:
```gleam
let #(a, _b) = #(x, 3)
```

This uses pattern matching on a tuple and generates:
```javascript
let $1 = [x, 3];    // Another assignment using the same counter! But counter wasn't reset
```

The problem is that `current_scope_vars` keeps a global counter for `$` across the entire function, and the counter never resets. But the case expression's internal variables (`$`, `$1`, etc.) are only used within that case expression's scope and then discarded - but the counter remains incremented.

Wait, let me re-read the PR description more carefully. The first example shows `$2` being duplicated:

```javascript
let $2 = $list.partition(...);  // first init
...
let $2 = $list.partition(...);  // second init - ERROR
```

And the minimal reproduction:
```javascript
let $1 = $[0];   // first init
...
let $1 = [x, 3]; // second init - ERROR
```

So the bug is that the `$` counter is shared across the function and when we have multiple case/pattern expressions, they reuse the same counter and produce duplicate variable names.

  1850	fn assign_subject<'a>(
  1851	    expression_generator: &mut Generator<'_, 'a>,
  1852	    subject: &'a TypedExpr,
  1853	    ordering: Ordering,
  1854	) -> SubjectAssignment<'a> {
  1855	    static ASSIGNMENT_VAR_ECO_STR: OnceLock<EcoString> = OnceLock::new();
  1856	
  1857	    // If the value is a variable we don't need to assign it to a new
  1858	    // variable, we can use the value expression safely without worrying about
  1859	    // performing computation or side effects multiple times.
  1860	    if let TypedExpr::Var {
  1861	        name, constructor, ..
  1862	    } = subject
  1863	        && constructor.is_local_variable()
  1864	    {
  1865	        SubjectAssignment::AlreadyAVariable {
  1866	            name: expression_generator.local_var(name),
  1867	        }
  1868	    } else {
  1869	        // If it's not a variable we need to assign it to a variable
  1870	        // to avoid rendering the subject expression multiple times
  1871	        let name = expression_generator
  1872	            .next_local_var(ASSIGNMENT_VAR_ECO_STR.get_or_init(|| ASSIGNMENT_VAR.into()));
  1873	        let value = expression_generator
  1874	            .not_in_tail_position(Some(ordering), |this| this.wrap_expression(subject));
  1875	
  1876	        SubjectAssignment::BindToVariable { value, name }
  1877	    }
  1878	}
  1879	
  1880	fn assignments_to_doc(assignments: Vec<SubjectAssignment<'_>>) -> Document<'_> {
  1881	    let mut assignments_docs = vec![];
  1882	    for assignment in assignments.into_iter() {
  1883	        let SubjectAssignment::BindToVariable { name, value } = assignment else {
  1884	            continue;
  1885	        };
  1886	        assignments_docs.push(docvec![let_doc(name, value), line()])
  1887	    }
  1888	    assignments_docs.to_doc()
  1889	}
  1890	
  1891	/// Appends the second document to the first one separating the two with a newline.
  1892	/// However, if the second document is empty the empty line is not added.
  1893	///
  1894	fn join_with_line<'a>(one: Document<'a>, other: Document<'a>) -> Document<'a> {
  1895	    if one.is_empty() {
  1896	        other
  1897	    } else if other.is_empty() {
  1898	        one
  1899	    } else {
  1900	        docvec![one, line(), other]
  1901	    }
  1902	}
  1903	
  1904	fn reassignment_doc(variable_name: EcoString, value: Document<'_>) -> Document<'_> {
  1905	    docvec![variable_name, " = ", value, ";"]
  1906	}

  1908	fn let_doc(variable_name: EcoString, value: Document<'_>) -> Document<'_> {

2026-05-31 09:38:27,735 - INFO - swea-agent-gleam-lang__gleam-5493 - 💭 THOUGHT
Now I see the issue! In `assign_subject` (line 1850-1878), when the subject is not already a variable, it calls `expression_generator.next_local_var(ASSIGNMENT_VAR_ECO_STR...)` which uses the name `"$"`. 

This increments the counter in `current_scope_vars` for `"$"` every time a new subject needs to be bound. But when we have multiple case expressions or let-assert patterns in the same function, they all share the same counter, leading to duplicate variable names like `$1` being declared twice.

But wait - let me look more carefully at the code. The `Variables` struct has its own scoping mechanism with `scoped_variable_names`, but the variable counter in `expression_generator.current_scope_vars` is global to the function. 

Let me check how the scoping works in `inside_new_scope`:

Looking at lines 602-633, `inside_new_scope` saves and restores:
- `expression_generator.current_scope_vars` (only for `DecisionKind::Case`)
- `self.variables.scoped_variable_names`
- `self.variables.segment_values`
- `self.variables.scoped_segment_names`

So for `DecisionKind::Case`, the `current_scope_vars` IS saved and restored. But for `DecisionKind::LetAssert`, it is NOT saved and restored.

But wait, the problem in the PR description shows a case expression followed by a let-pattern (tuple destructuring):

```gleam
pub fn main() {
  let x =
    case #(1, 2) {
      #(1, b) -> b
      #(_, b) -> b
    }

  let #(a, _b) = #(x, 3)

  a
}
```

The `case` expression generates `$` and `$1` for the tuple subjects. Then the `let #(a, _b) = #(x, 3)` is a let-assignment, not a case expression. Let me check how tuple patterns in let assignments are compiled.

Let me search for how let assignments with tuple patterns are compiled

   848	    fn statements(&mut self, statements: &'a [TypedStatement]) -> Document<'a> {
   849	        // If there are any statements that need to be printed at statement level, that's
   850	        // for an outer scope so we don't want to print them inside this one.
   851	        let statement_level = std::mem::take(&mut self.statement_level);
   852	        let count = statements.len();
   853	        let mut documents = Vec::with_capacity(count * 3);
   854	        for (i, statement) in statements.iter().enumerate() {
   855	            if i + 1 < count {
   856	                let function_position =
   857	                    std::mem::replace(&mut self.function_position, Position::Statement);
   858	                let scope_position =
   859	                    std::mem::replace(&mut self.scope_position, Position::Statement);
   860	
   861	                documents.push(self.statement(statement));
   862	
   863	                self.function_position = function_position;
   864	                self.scope_position = scope_position;
   865	
   866	                if requires_semicolon(statement) {
   867	                    documents.push(";".to_doc());
   868	                }
   869	                documents.push(line());
   870	            } else {
   871	                documents.push(self.statement(statement));
   872	            }
   873	
   874	            // If we've generated code for a statement that always throws, we
   875	            // can skip code generation for all the following ones.
   876	            if self.let_assert_always_panics {
   877	                self.let_assert_always_panics = false;
   878	                break;
   879	            }
   880	        }
   881	        self.statement_level = statement_level;
   882	        if count == 1 {
   883	            documents.to_doc()
   884	        } else {
   885	            documents.to_doc().force_break()
   886	        }
   887	    }
   888	
   889	    fn simple_variable_assignment(
   890	        &mut self,
   891	        name: &'a EcoString,
   892	        value: &'a TypedExpr,
   893	    ) -> Document<'a> {
   894	        // Subject must be rendered before the variable for variable numbering
   895	        let subject =
   896	            self.not_in_tail_position(Some(Ordering::Loose), |this| this.wrap_expression(value));
   897	        let js_name = self.next_local_var(name);
   898	        let assignment = docvec!["let ", js_name.clone(), " = ", subject, ";"];
   899	        let assignment = match &self.scope_position {
   900	            Position::Expression(_) | Position::Statement => assignment,
   901	            Position::Tail => docvec![assignment, line(), "return ", js_name, ";"],
   902	            Position::Assign(block_variable) => docvec![
   903	                assignment,
   904	                line(),
   905	                block_variable.clone(),
   906	                " = ",
   907	                js_name,
   908	                ";"
   909	            ],
   910	        };
   911	
   912	        assignment.force_break()
   913	    }
   914	
   915	    fn assignment(&mut self, assignment: &'a TypedAssignment) -> Document<'a> {
   916	        let TypedAssignment {
   917	            pattern,
   918	            kind,
   919	            value,
   920	            compiled_case,
   921	            annotation: _,
   922	            location: _,
   923	        } = assignment;
   924	
   925	        // In case the pattern is just a variable, we special case it to
   926	        // generate just a simple assignment instead of using the decision tree
   927	        // for the code generation step.
   928	        if let TypedPattern::Variable { name, .. } = pattern {
   929	            return self.simple_variable_assignment(name, value);
   930	        }
   931	
   932	        decision::let_(compiled_case, value, kind, self, pattern)
   933	    }
   934	
   935	    fn assert(&mut self, assert: &'a TypedAssert) -> Document<'a> {
   936	        let TypedAssert {
   937	            location,
   938	            value,
   939	            message,
   940	        } = assert;
   941	
   942	        let message = match message {
   943	            Some(message) => {
   944	                self.not_in_tail_position(Some(Ordering::Strict), |this| this.expression(message))
   945	            }
   946	            None => string("Assertion failed."),
   947	        };
   948	
   949	        let check = self.not_in_tail_position(Some(Ordering::Loose), |this| {
   950	            this.assert_check(value, &message, *location)

  1731	    /// A runtime check might need to reference some bit array segments in its
  1732	    /// check (for example if a bit array length depends on a previous segment).
  1733	    /// This function returns a vector with all the assignments needed to bring
  1734	    /// the referenced segments into scope, so they're available to use for the
  1735	    /// runtime check.
  1736	    ///
  1737	    fn bit_array_segment_assignments(&mut self, check: &RuntimeCheck) -> Vec<Document<'a>> {
  1738	        let mut check_assignments = vec![];
  1739	        for (segment, _) in check.referenced_segment_patterns() {
  1740	            // If the segment was already bound to a variable in this scope we
  1741	            // don't need to generate any further assignment for it. We will just
  1742	            // reuse that existing variable when we need to access this segment
  1743	            if self.segment_is_bound_in_scope(segment) {
  1744	                continue;
  1745	            }
  1746	
  1747	            let variable_name = self.next_local_var(segment);
  1748	            let segment_value = self
  1749	                .get_segment_value(segment)
  1750	                .expect("segment referenced in a check before being created");
  1751	            self.bind_segment(variable_name.clone(), segment.clone());
  1752	            check_assignments.push(let_doc(variable_name, segment_value))
  1753	        }
  1754	        check_assignments
  1755	    }

  1757	    /// Returns a string representing the value of a pattern variable: it might
  1758	    /// be the code needed to obtain such variable (for example accessing a
  1759	    /// list item `wibble.head`), or it could be a name this variable was bound
  1760	    /// to in the current scope to avoid doing any repeated work!
  1761	    ///
  1762	    fn get_value(&self, variable: &Variable) -> EcoString {
  1763	        // If the pattern variable was already assigned to a variable that is
  1764	        // in scope we use that variable name!
  1765	        if let Some(name) = self.scoped_variable_names.get(&variable.id) {
  1766	            return name.clone();
  1767	        }
  1768	
  1769	        // Otherwise we fallback to using its value directly.
  1770	        self.variable_values
  1771	            .get(&variable.id)
  1772	            .expect("pattern variable used before assignment")
  1773	            .clone()
  1774	    }
  1775	
  1776	    fn get_segment_value(&self, segment_name: &EcoString) -> Option<Document<'a>> {
  1777	        // If the segment was already assigned to a variable that is in scope
  1778	        // we use that variable name!
  1779	        if let Some(name) = self.scoped_segment_names.get(segment_name) {
  1780	            return Some(name.clone().to_doc());
  1781	        }
  1782	
  1783	        // Otherwise we fallback to using its value directly.
  1784	        self.segment_values.get(segment_name).cloned()
  1785	    }
  1786	}
  1787	
  1788	/// When going over the subjects of a case expression/let we might end up in two
  1789	/// situation: the subject might be a variable or it could be a more complex
  1790	/// expression (like a function call, a complex expression, ...).
  1791	///
  1792	/// ```gleam
  1793	/// case a_variable { ... }
  1794	/// case a_function_call(wobble) { ... }
  1795	/// ```
  1796	///
  1797	/// When checking on a case we might end up repeating the subjects multiple times
  1798	/// (as they need to appear in various checks), this means that if we ended up
  1799	/// doing the simple thing of just repeating the subject as it is, we might end
  1800	/// up dramatically changing the meaning of the program when the subject is a
  1801	/// complex expression! Imagine this example:
  1802	///
  1803	/// ```gleam
  1804	/// case wibble("a") {
  1805	///   1 -> todo
  1806	///   2 -> todo
  1807	///   _ -> todo
  1808	/// }
  1809	/// ```
  1810	///
  1811	/// If we just repeated the subject every time we need to check it, the decision
  1812	/// tree would end up looking something like this:
  1813	///
  1814	/// ```js
  1815	/// if (wibble("a") === 1) {}
  1816	/// else if (wibble("a") === 2) {}
  1817	/// else {}
  1818	/// ```
  1819	///
  1820	/// It would be quite bad as we would end up running the same function multiple
  1821	/// times instead of just once!
  1822	///
  1823	/// So we need to split each subject in two categories: if it is a simple
  1824	/// variable already, it's no big deal and we can repeat that name as many times
  1825	/// as we want; however, if it's anything else we first need to bind that subject
  1826	/// to a variable we can then reference multiple times.
  1827	///
  1828	enum SubjectAssignment<'a> {
  1829	    /// The subject is a complex expression with a `value` that has to be
  1830	    /// assigned to a variable with the given `name` as repeating the `value`
  1831	    /// multiple times could possibly change the meaning of the program.
  1832	    BindToVariable {
  1833	        name: EcoString,
  1834	        value: Document<'a>,
  1835	    },
  1836	    /// The subject is already a simple variable with the given name, we will
  1837	    /// keep using that name to reference it.
  1838	    AlreadyAVariable { name: EcoString },
  1839	}
  1840	
  1841	impl SubjectAssignment<'_> {
  1842	    fn name(&self) -> EcoString {
  1843	        match self {
  1844	            SubjectAssignment::BindToVariable { name, value: _ }
  1845	            | SubjectAssignment::AlreadyAVariable { name } => name.clone(),
  1846	        }
  1847	    }
  1848	}
  1849

Then `assign_let_subject` calls `assign_subject`, which calls `next_local_var("$")`. This increments the counter.\n\nThen, the decision tree is generated. But the decision tree variables (like `$1 = $[0]`) are generated inside `inside_new_scope` and those increments are rolled back.\n\nWait, but where does `$1` come from in the case expression? Then `record_check_assignments` is called for the tuple check, which sets values for elements: `$[0]` and `$[1]`.\n\n4. Let me check...\n\nActually, I think the `$1` comes from the `switch` function where it checks if the variable needs to be bound:\n\n```rust\nif !self.variables.is_bound_in_scope(var) {\n    let name = self.variables.next_local_var(&ASSIGNMENT_VAR.into());\n    // ...\n}\n```\n\nBut this is for the main `var` which is already bound from `assign_case_subjects`. So this shouldn\'t fire.\n\nHowever, there\'s the `first_character_variable` which also uses `next_local_var`.\n\nBut looking at the generated code from the PR:\n```javascript\nlet $ = [1, 2];\nlet $1 = $[0];\nif ($1 === 1) { ... }\n```\n\nSo `$` is the subject (the tuple), and `$1` is `$[0]` (first element). 2026-05-31 09:39:18,307 - DEBUG - swea-lm-gleam-lang__gleam-5493 - total_tokens_sent=469,870, total_tokens_received=3,671, total_cost=0.00, total_api_calls=22
2026-05-31 09:39:18,309 - INFO - swea-agent-gleam-lang__gleam-5493 - 💭 THOUGHT
Now I have a clear picture. Let me trace the issue:

In `assign_subject` (line 1850-1878), when the subject is not already a variable, it calls `next_local_var` with `"$"`. This increments the counter in `current_scope_vars`.

In `inside_new_scope` (line 602-633), for `DecisionKind::Case`, the `current_scope_vars` IS saved and restored. So after a case expression, the counter should be restored.

But wait! There's also the `Variables::new` which does NOT save/restore the scope. And the `assign_subject` function is called before any `inside_new_scope` call. Let me look at the flow for case:

1. `case()` (line 28-84):
   - `Variables::new(expression_generator, VariableAssignment::Declare)` - no scope save
   - `variables.assign_case_subjects(compiled_case, subjects)` - may call `assign_subject` → `next_local_var("$")`
   - Then `CasePrinter` is created with the variables
   - `printer.decision(tree)` is called
   - Inside decision → switch → `inside_new_scope` saves/restores `current_scope_vars`

So the `assign_case_subjects` happens OUTSIDE of `inside_new_scope`. The assignments from `assign_case_subjects` increment the counter, and that counter is not reset.

BUT, in the `case()` function at lines 83-84:
```rust
docvec![assignments_to_doc(assignments), decision].force_break()
```

The assignments are emitted at the top level. The counter has been incremented, but `inside_new_scope` should restore it for the decision part. However, the assignments themselves use the counter and the names are used in the outer scope.

Now, the problem: let's say the first `case` expression creates `$` (counter 0→1) and `$1` (counter 1→2). After the case expression, `inside_new_scope` restores `current_scope_vars` to what it was before the decision tree... but the assignments have already been generated with those names.

Wait, actually, let me re-read `inside_new_scope` more carefully:

```rust
fn inside_new_scope<A, F>(&mut self, run: F) -> A
where
    F: Fn(&mut Self) -> A,
{
    let old_scope = match &self.kind {
        DecisionKind::Case { .. } => self
            .variables
            .expression_generator
            .current_scope_vars
            .clone(),
        DecisionKind::LetAssert { .. } => Default::default(),
    };
    // ...
    let output = run(self);
    match &self.kind {
        DecisionKind::Case { .. } => {
            self.variables.expression_generator.current_scope_vars = old_scope
        }
        DecisionKind::LetAssert { .. } => {}
    }
    // ...
    output
}
```

For `Case`, it saves and restores `current_scope_vars`. So the counter increments from `assign_case_subjects` would persist, but any increments inside the decision tree would be rolled back.

Hmm, but the minimal reproduction shows:
```javascript
let $ = [1, 2];
let $1 = $[0];  // first init of $1
...
let $1 = [x, 3]; // second init of $1 - ERROR
```

So `$` and `$1` are both used in the first case expression, and then `$1` is reused for the second let-pattern. This means:
- After first case: counter for `$` is at 2 (used `$` and `$1`)
- For the second assignment (which is a let with tuple pattern), the counter is NOT reset because `let_` uses `DecisionKind::LetAssert` which does NOT save/restore the scope.

Wait, but the second one is just a `let`, not a `let assert`. Let me check what happens for `let` assignments with patterns:

Looking at `assignment()` at line 915-933:
```rust
fn assignment(&mut self, assignment: &'a TypedAssignment) -> Document<'a> {
    // ...
    if let TypedPattern::Variable { name, .. } = pattern {
        return self.simple_variable_assignment(name, value);
    }
    decision::let_(compiled_case, value, kind, self, pattern)
}
```

For a let with a tuple pattern (like `let #(a, _b) = #(x, 3)`), it falls through to `decision::let_`. Let me look at that:

```rust
pub fn let_<'a>(
    compiled_case: &'a CompiledCase,
    subject: &'a TypedExpr,
    kind: &'a AssignmentKind<TypedExpr>,
    expression_generator: &mut Generator<'_, 'a>,
    pattern: &'a TypedPattern,
) -> Document<'a> {
    let scope_position = expression_generator.scope_position.clone();
    let mut variables = Variables::new(expression_generator, VariableAssignment::Reassign);

    let assignment = variables.assign_let_subject(compiled_case, subject);
    // ...
}
```

`Variables::new` is called with `VariableAssignment::Reassign`. Then `assign_let_subject` calls `assign_subject`, which calls `next_local_var("$")`. This increments the counter.

Then, the decision tree is generated. Inside the decision tree, `inside_new_scope` is called, but for `LetAssert` it does NOT save/restore `current_scope_vars`!

So the counter persists and keeps incrementing.

But wait, there's another issue. Even for `Case`, the counter from `assign_case_subjects` happens BEFORE `inside_new_scope` is called. Let me check:

`case()`:
1. `variables.assign_case_subjects(...)` - this calls `next_local_var` if needed, incrementing counter
2. `CasePrinter { variables, ... }` created
3. `printer.decision(tree)` called
4. Inside `switch()`, `inside_new_scope` saves/restores

So step 1 happens outside the scope save. The counter increments from step 1 are permanent. But the decision tree variables (like `$1 = $[0]`) are generated inside `inside_new_scope` and those increments are rolled back.

Wait, but where does `$1` come from in the case expression? Let me trace more carefully.

For `case #(1, 2) { ... }`:
1. `assign_case_subjects` creates subject assignment for the tuple `#(1, 2)`. It calls `next_local_var("$")` → counter becomes 1, name is `$` (since first call returns the base name when counter was 0).

2. Then in `switch()`, the first thing checked is whether the variable is bound. It was just bound in step 1, so `is_bound_in_scope` returns true. 

3. Then `record_check_assignments` is called for the tuple check, which sets values for elements: `$[0]` and `$[1]`.

4. Then for each choice (matching `#(1, b)` and `#(_, b)`), inside `inside_new_scope`:
   - For the first choice: it needs to check `$[0] === 1`. But wait, is `$[0]` already bound? Let me check...

Actually, I think the `$1` comes from the `switch` function where it checks if the variable needs to be bound:

```rust
if !self.variables.is_bound_in_scope(var) {
    let name = self.variables.next_local_var(&ASSIGNMENT_VAR.into());
    // ...
}
```

But this is for the main `var` which is already bound from `assign_case_subjects`. So this shouldn't fire.

However, there's the `first_character_variable` which also uses `next_local_var`.

But looking at the generated code from the PR:
```javascript
let $ = [1, 2];
let $1 = $[0];
if ($1 === 1) { ... }
```

So `$` is the subject (the tuple), and `$1` is `$[0]` (first element). Where does `$1` come from?

I think it comes from the `switch` function's `first_character_variable` or from the check itself... wait, no. Let me look at the tuple check. For tuple patterns, the decision tree would have a `Switch` on the tuple variable, with choices for each tuple pattern. But actually, for tuples, the check is a `Tuple` runtime check at the `Switch` level.

Wait, actually I need to understand how the decision tree works for tuples. Let me look at the exhaustiveness module.

Actually, let me take a different approach. Let me first create a reproduction script to confirm the bug.

     5	export function read_file(path) {
     6	  try {
     7	    return Result$Ok(readFileSync(path));
     8	  } catch {
     9	    return Result$Error(undefined);
    10	  }
    11	}
    12	
    13	async function* gleamFiles(directory) {
    14	  for (let entry of await read_dir(directory)) {
    15	    let path = join_path(directory, entry);
    16	    if (path.endsWith(".gleam")) {
    17	      yield path;
    18	    } else {
    19	      try {
    20	        yield* gleamFiles(path);
    21	      } catch (error) {
    22	        // Could not read directory, assume it's a file
    23	      }
    24	    }
    25	  }
    26	}
    27	
    28	async function readRootPackageName() {
    29	  let toml = await async_read_file("gleam.toml", "utf-8");
    30	  for (let line of toml.split("\n")) {
    31	    let matches = line.match(/\s*name\s*=\s*"([a-z][a-z0-9_]*)"/); // Match regexp in compiler-cli/src/new.rs in validate_name()
    32	    if (matches) return matches[1];
    33	  }
    34	  throw new Error("Could not determine package name from gleam.toml");
    35	}
    36	
    37	export async function main() {
    38	  let state = reporting.new_state();
    39	
    40	  let packageName = await readRootPackageName();
    41	  let dist = `../${packageName}/`;
    42	
    43	  for await (let path of await gleamFiles("test")) {
    44	    let js_path = path.slice("test/".length).replace(".gleam", ".mjs");
    45	    let module = await import(join_path(dist, js_path));
    46	    for (let fnName of Object.keys(module)) {
    47	      if (!fnName.endsWith("_test")) continue;
    48	      try {
    49	        await module[fnName]();
    50	        state = reporting.test_passed(state);
    51	      } catch (error) {
    52	        let moduleName = js_path.slice(0, -4);
    53	        state = reporting.test_failed(state, moduleName, fnName, error);
    54	      }
    55	    }
    56	  }
    57	
    58	  const status = reporting.finished(state);
    59	  exit(status);
    60	}
    61	
    62	export function crash(message) {
    63	  throw new Error(message);
    64	}
    65	
    66	function exit(code) {
    67	  if (globalThis.Deno) {
    68	    Deno.exit(code);
    69	  } else {
    70	    process.exit(code);
    71	  }
    72	}
    73	
    74	async function read_dir(path) {
    75	  if (globalThis.Deno) {
    76	    let items = [];
    77	    for await (let item of Deno.readDir(path, { withFileTypes: true })) {
    78	      items.push(item.name);
    79	    }
    80	    return items;
    81	  } else {
    82	    let { readdir } = await import("node:fs/promises");
    83	    return readdir(path);
    84	  }
    85	}
    86	
    87	function join_path(a, b) {
    88	  if (a.endsWith("/")) return a + b;
    89	  return a + "/" + b;
    90	}
    91	
    92	async function async_read_file(path) {
    93	  if (globalThis.Deno) {
    94	    return Deno.readTextFile(path);
    95	  } else {
    96	    let { readFile } = await import("node:fs/promises");
    97	    let contents = await readFile(path);
    98	    return contents.toString();
    99	  }

    36	function gleam_to_erlang_module_name(path) {
    37	  let $ = $string.ends_with(path, ".gleam");
    38	  if ($) {
    39	    let _pipe = path;
    40	    let _pipe$1 = $string.replace(_pipe, ".gleam", "");
    41	    return $string.replace(_pipe$1, "/", "@");
    42	  } else {
    43	    let _pipe = path;
    44	    let _pipe$1 = $string.split(_pipe, "/");
    45	    let _pipe$2 = $list.last(_pipe$1);
    46	    let _pipe$3 = $result.unwrap(_pipe$2, path);
    47	    return $string.replace(_pipe$3, ".erl", "");
    48	  }
    49	}

    51	/**
    52	 * Find and run all test functions for the current project using Erlang's EUnit
    53	 * test framework, or a custom JavaScript test runner.
    54	 *
    55	 * Any Erlang or Gleam function in the `test` directory with a name ending in
    56	 * `_test` is considered a test function and will be run.
    57	 *
    58	 * A test that panics is considered a failure.
    59	 */

    17	export function from_dynamic(error) {
    18	  if (!(error instanceof globalThis.Error) || !error.gleam_error) {
    19	    return Result$Error(undefined);
    20	  }
    21	
    22	  if (error.gleam_error === "todo") {
    23	    return wrap(error, PanicKind$Todo());
    24	  }
    25	
    26	  if (error.gleam_error === "panic") {
    27	    return wrap(error, PanicKind$Panic());
    28	  }
    29	
    30	  if (error.gleam_error === "let_assert") {
    31	    let kind = PanicKind$LetAssert(
    32	      error.start,
    33	      error.end,
    34	      error.pattern_start,
    35	      error.pattern_end,
    36	      error.value,
    37	    );
    38	    return wrap(error, kind);
    39	  }
    40	
    41	  if (error.gleam_error === "assert") {
    42	    let kind = PanicKind$Assert(
    43	      error.start,
    44	      error.end,
    45	      error.expression_start,
    46	      assert_kind(error),
    47	    );
    48	    return wrap(error, kind);
    49	  }
    50	
    51	  return Result$Error(undefined);
    52	}
    53	
    54	function assert_kind(error) {
    55	  if (error.kind == "binary_operator") {
    56	    return AssertKind$BinaryOperator(
    57	      error.operator,
    58	      expression(error.left),
    59	      expression(error.right),
    60	    );
    61	  }
    62	
    63	  if (error.kind == "function_call") {
    64	    let list = List$Empty();
    65	    let i = error.arguments.length;
    66	    while (i--) {
    67	      list = List$NonEmpty(expression(error.arguments[i]), list);
    68	    }
    69	    return AssertKind$FunctionCall(list);
    70	  }
    71	
    72	  return AssertKind$OtherExpression(expression(error.expression));
    73	}
    74	
    75	function expression(data) {
    76	  const expression = AssertedExpression$AssertedExpression(data.start, data.end, undefined);
    77	  if (data.kind == "literal") {
    78	    expression.kind = ExpressionKind$Literal(data.value);
    79	  } else if (data.kind == "expression") {
    80	    expression.kind = ExpressionKind$Expression(data.value);
    81	  } else {
    82	    expression.kind = ExpressionKind$Unevaluated();
    83	  }
    84	  return expression;
    85	}

    87	function wrap(e, kind) {
    88	  return Result$Ok(
    89	    GleamPanic$GleamPanic(e.message, e.file, e.module, e.function, e.line, kind),
    90	  );
    91	}

    35	function bold(text) {
    36	  return ("\u{001b}[1m" + text) + "\u{001b}[22m";
    37	}

    39	function cyan(text) {
    40	  return ("\u{001b}[36m" + text) + "\u{001b}[39m";

    43	function code_snippet(src, start, end) {
    44	  let _pipe = $result.try$(
    45	    $option.to_result(src, undefined),
    46	    (src) => {
    47	      return $result.try$(
    48	        $bit_array.slice(src, start, end - start),
    49	        (snippet) => {
    50	          return $result.try$(
    51	            $bit_array.to_string(snippet),
    52	            (snippet) => {
    53	              let snippet$1 = ((cyan(" code") + ": ") + snippet) + "\n";
    54	              return new Ok(snippet$1);
    55	            },
    56	          );
    57	        },
    58	      );
    59	    },
    60	  );
    61	  return $result.unwrap(_pipe, "");
    62	}

    69	  $io.print(((("\n" + module) + ".") + function$) + yellow(" skipped"));
    70	  return new State(state.passed, state.failed, state.skipped + 1);
    71	}

    86	export function finished(state) {
    87	  let $ = state.failed;
    88	  if ($ === 0) {
    89	    let $1 = state.skipped;
    90	    if ($1 === 0) {
    91	      let $2 = state.passed;
    92	      if ($2 === 0) {
    93	        $io.println("\nNo tests found!");
    94	        return 1;
    95	      } else {
    96	        let message = ("\n" + $int.to_string(state.passed)) + " passed, no failures";
    97	        $io.println(green(message));
    98	        return 0;
    99	      }
   100	    } else {
   101	      let message = ((("\n" + $int.to_string(state.passed)) + " passed, 0 failures, ") + $int.to_string(
   102	        state.skipped,
   103	      )) + " skipped";
   104	      $io.println(yellow(message));
   105	      return 1;
   106	    }
   107	  } else {
   108	    let $1 = state.skipped;
   109	    if ($1 === 0) {
   110	      let message = ((("\n" + $int.to_string(state.passed)) + " passed, ") + $int.to_string(
   111	        state.failed,
   112	      )) + " failures";
   113	      $io.println(red(message));
   114	      return 1;
   115	    } else {
   116	      let message = ((((("\n" + $int.to_string(state.passed)) + " passed, ") + $int.to_string(
   117	        state.failed,
   118	      )) + " failures, ") + $int.to_string(state.skipped)) + " skipped";
   119	      $io.println(red(message));
   120	      return 1;
   121	    }
   122	  }
   123	}

   125	export function eunit_missing() {
   126	  let message = bold(red("Error")) + ": EUnit libraries not found.\n\nYour Erlang installation seems to be incomplete. If you installed Erlang using\na package manager ensure that you have installed the full Erlang\ndistribution instead of a stripped-down version.\n";
   127	  $io.print_error(message);
   128	  return new Error(undefined);
   129	}

   135	function format_unknown(module, function$, error) {
   136	  return $string.concat(
   137	    toList([
   138	      grey((module + ".") + function$) + "\n",
   139	      "An unexpected error occurred:\n",
   140	      "\n",
   141	      ("  " + $string.inspect(error)) + "\n",
   142	    ]),
   143	  );
   144	}

   146	function inspect_value(value) {
   147	  let $ = value.kind;
   148	  if ($ instanceof $gleam_panic.Literal) {
   149	    return grey("literal");
   150	  } else if ($ instanceof $gleam_panic.Expression) {
   151	    let value$1 = $.value;
   152	    return $string.inspect(value$1);
   153	  } else {
   154	    return grey("unevaluated");
   155	  }
   156	}
   157	
   158	function assert_value(name, value) {
   159	  return ((cyan(name) + ": ") + inspect_value(value)) + "\n";
   160	}
   161	
   162	function assert_info(kind) {
   163	  if (kind instanceof $gleam_panic.BinaryOperator) {
   164	    let left = kind.left;
   165	    let right = kind.right;
   166	    return $string.concat(
   167	      toList([assert_value(" left", left), assert_value("right", right)]),
   168	    );
   169	  } else if (kind instanceof $gleam_panic.FunctionCall) {
   170	    let arguments$ = kind.arguments;
   171	    let _pipe = arguments$;
   172	    let _pipe$1 = $list.index_map(
   173	      _pipe,
   174	      (e, i) => {
   175	        let number = $string.pad_start($int.to_string(i), 5, " ");
   176	        return assert_value(number, e);
   177	      },
   178	    );
   179	    return $string.concat(_pipe$1);
   180	  } else {
   181	    return "";
   182	  }
   183	}
   184	
   185	function format_gleam_error(error, module, function$, src) {
   186	  let location = grey((error.file + ":") + $int.to_string(error.line));
   187	  let $ = error.kind;
   188	  if ($ instanceof $gleam_panic.Todo) {
   189	    return $string.concat(
   190	      toList([
   191	        ((bold(yellow("todo")) + " ") + location) + "\n",
   192	        ((((cyan(" test") + ": ") + module) + ".") + function$) + "\n",
   193	        ((cyan(" info") + ": ") + error.message) + "\n",
   194	      ]),
   195	    );
   196	  } else if ($ instanceof $gleam_panic.Panic) {
   197	    return $string.concat(
   198	      toList([
   199	        ((bold(red("panic")) + " ") + location) + "\n",
   200	        ((((cyan(" test") + ": ") + module) + ".") + function$) + "\n",
   201	        ((cyan(" info") + ": ") + error.message) + "\n",
   202	      ]),
   203	    );
   204	  } else if ($ instanceof $gleam_panic.LetAssert) {
   205	    let start = $.start;
   206	    let end = $.end;
   207	    let value = $.value;
   208	    return $string.concat(
   209	      toList([
   210	        ((bold(red("let assert")) + " ") + location) + "\n",
   211	        ((((cyan(" test") + ": ") + module) + ".") + function$) + "\n",
   212	        code_snippet(src, start, end),
   213	        ((cyan("value") + ": ") + $string.inspect(value)) + "\n",
   214	        ((cyan(" info") + ": ") + error.message) + "\n",
   215	      ]),
   216	    );
   217	  } else {
   218	    let start = $.start;
   219	    let end = $.end;
   220	    let kind = $.kind;
   221	    return $string.concat(
   222	      toList([
   223	        ((bold(red("assert")) + " ") + location) + "\n",
   224	        ((((cyan(" test") + ": ") + module) + ".") + function$) + "\n",
   225	        code_snippet(src, start, end),
   226	        assert_info(kind),
   227	        ((cyan(" info") + ": ") + error.message) + "\n",
   228	      ]),
   229	    );
   230	  }
   231	}
   232	
   233	function read_file(path) {
   234	  let $ = read_file_text(path);
   235	  if ($ instanceof Ok) {
   236	    let text = $[0];
   237	    return new Ok($bit_array.from_string(text));
   238	  } else {
   239	    return $;
   240	  }
   241	}
   242	
   243	export function test_failed(state, module, function$, error) {
   244	  let _block;
   245	  let $ = $gleam_panic.from_dynamic(error);
   246	  if ($ instanceof Ok) {
   247	    let error$1 = $[0];
   248	    let src = $option.from_result(read_file(error$1.file));
   249	    _block = format_gleam_error(error$1, module, function$, src);
   250	  } else {
   251	    _block = format_unknown(module, function$, error);
   252	  }
   253	  let message = _block;
   254	  $io.print("\n" + message);
   255	  return new State(state.passed, state.failed + 1, state.skipped);
   256	}

     9	  let $ = isEqual(a, b);
    10	  if ($) {
    11	    return undefined;
    12	  } else {
    13	    throw makeError(
    14	      "panic",
    15	      FILEPATH,
    16	      "gleeunit/should",
    17	      10,

    20	        toList([
    21	          "\n",
    22	          $string.inspect(a),
    23	          "\nshould equal\n",

    28	    )

   124	  let _pipe = actual;
   125	  return equal(_pipe, true);

    95	export function int_from_base_string(string, base) {
    96	  if (int_base_patterns[base].test(string.replace(/^-/, "").toLowerCase())) {
    97	    return Result$Error(Nil);
    98	  }
    99	
   100	  const result = parseInt(string, base);
   101	
   102	  if (isNaN(result)) {
   103	    return Result$Error(Nil);
   104	  }
   105	
   106	  return Result$Ok(result);
   107	}

   117	export function string_length(string) {
   118	  if (string === "") {
   119	    return 0;
   120	  }
   121	  const iterator = graphemes_iterator(string);
   122	  if (iterator) {
   123	    let i = 0;
   124	    for (const _ of iterator) {
   125	      i++;
   126	    }
   127	    return i;
   128	  } else {
   129	    return string.match(/./gsu).length;
   130	  }
   131	}
   132	
   133	export function graphemes(string) {
   134	  const iterator = graphemes_iterator(string);
   135	  if (iterator) {
   136	    return arrayToList(Array.from(iterator).map((item) => item.segment));
   137	  } else {
   138	    return arrayToList(string.match(/./gsu));
   139	  }
   140	}
   141	
   142	let segmenter = undefined;
   143	
   144	function graphemes_iterator(string) {
   145	  if (globalThis.Intl && Intl.Segmenter) {
   146	    segmenter ||= new Intl.Segmenter();
   147	    return segmenter.segment(string)[Symbol.iterator]();
   148	  }
   149	}

   151	export function pop_grapheme(string) {
   152	  let first;
   153	  const iterator = graphemes_iterator(string);
   154	  if (iterator) {
   155	    first = iterator.next().value?.segment;
   156	  } else {
   157	    first = string.match(/./su)?.[0];
   158	  }
   159	  if (first) {
   160	    return Result$Ok([first, string.slice(first.length)]);
   161	  } else {
   162	    return Result$Error(Nil);
   163	  }
   164	}

   206	export function string_grapheme_slice(string, idx, len) {
   207	  if (len <= 0 || idx >= string.length) {
   208	    return "";
   209	  }
   210	
   211	  const iterator = graphemes_iterator(string);
   212	  if (iterator) {
   213	    while (idx-- > 0) {
   214	      iterator.next();
   215	    }
   216	
   217	    let result = "";
   218	
   219	    while (len-- > 0) {
   220	      const v = iterator.next().value;
   221	      if (v === undefined) {
   222	        break;
   223	      }
   224	
   225	      result += v.segment;
   226	    }
   227	
   228	    return result;
   229	  } else {
   230	    return string
   231	      .match(/./gsu)
   232	      .slice(idx, idx + len)
   233	      .join("");
   234	  }
   235	}

   256	export function split_once(haystack, needle) {
   257	  const index = haystack.indexOf(needle);
   258	  if (index >= 0) {
   259	    const before = haystack.slice(0, index);
   260	    const after = haystack.slice(index + needle.length);
   261	    return Result$Ok([before, after]);
   262	  } else {
   263	    return Result$Error(Nil);
   264	  }
   265	}

   267	const unicode_whitespaces = [

   269	  "\u0009", // Horizontal tab
   270	  "\u000A", // Line feed

   272	  "\u000C", // Form feed
   273	  "\u000D", // Carriage return

   276	  "\u2029", // Paragraph separator
   277	].join("");

   280	  `^[${unicode_whitespaces}]*`,
   281	);
   282	const trim_end_regex = /* @__PURE__ */ new RegExp(`[${unicode_whitespaces}]*$`);

   296	export function bit_array_bit_size(bit_array) {

   298	}

   300	export function bit_array_byte_size(bit_array) {
   301	  return bit_array.byteSize;
   302	}
   303	
   304	export function bit_array_pad_to_bytes(bit_array) {
   305	  const trailingBitsCount = bit_array.bitSize % 8;
   306	
   307	  // If the bit array is a whole number of bytes it can be returned unchanged
   308	  if (trailingBitsCount === 0) {
   309	    return bit_array;
   310	  }
   311	
   312	  const finalByte = bit_array.byteAt(bit_array.byteSize - 1);
   313	
   314	  // The required final byte has its unused trailing bits set to zero
   315	  const unusedBitsCount = 8 - trailingBitsCount;
   316	  const correctFinalByte = (finalByte >> unusedBitsCount) << unusedBitsCount;
   317	
   318	  // If the unused bits in the final byte are already set to zero then the
   319	  // existing buffer can be re-used, avoiding a copy
   320	  if (finalByte === correctFinalByte) {
   321	    return new BitArray(
   322	      bit_array.rawBuffer,
   323	      bit_array.byteSize * 8,
   324	      bit_array.bitOffset,
   325	    );
   326	  }
   327	
   328	  // Copy the bit array into a new aligned buffer and set the correct final byte
   329	  const buffer = new Uint8Array(bit_array.byteSize);
   330	  for (let i = 0; i < buffer.length - 1; i++) {
   331	    buffer[i] = bit_array.byteAt(i);
   332	  }
   333	  buffer[buffer.length - 1] = correctFinalByte;
   334	
   335	  return new BitArray(buffer);
   336	}
   337	
   338	export function bit_array_concat(bit_arrays) {
   339	  return toBitArray(bit_arrays.toArray());
   340	}

   354	export function bit_array_to_string(bit_array) {
   355	  // If the bit array isn't a whole number of bytes then return an error
   356	  if (bit_array.bitSize % 8 !== 0) {
   357	    return Result$Error(Nil);
   358	  }
   359	
   360	  try {
   361	    const decoder = new TextDecoder("utf-8", { fatal: true });
   362	
   363	    if (bit_array.bitOffset === 0) {
   364	      return Result$Ok(decoder.decode(bit_array.rawBuffer));
   365	    } else {
   366	      // The input data isn't aligned, so copy it into a new aligned buffer so
   367	      // that TextDecoder can be used
   368	      const buffer = new Uint8Array(bit_array.byteSize);
   369	      for (let i = 0; i < buffer.length; i++) {
   370	        buffer[i] = bit_array.byteAt(i);
   371	      }
   372	      return Result$Ok(decoder.decode(buffer));
   373	    }
   374	  } catch {
   375	    return Result$Error(Nil);
   376	  }
   377	}
   378	
   379	export function print(string) {
   380	  if (typeof process === "object" && process.stdout?.write) {
   381	    process.stdout.write(string); // We can write without a trailing newline
   382	  } else if (typeof Deno === "object") {
   383	    Deno.stdout.writeSync(new TextEncoder().encode(string)); // We can write without a trailing newline
   384	  } else {
   385	    console.log(string); // We're in a browser. Newlines are mandated
   386	  }
   387	}
   388	
   389	export function print_error(string) {
   390	  if (typeof process === "object" && process.stderr?.write) {
   391	    process.stderr.write(string); // We can write without a trailing newline
   392	  } else if (typeof Deno === "object") {
   393	    Deno.stderr.writeSync(new TextEncoder().encode(string)); // We can write without a trailing newline
   394	  } else {
   395	    console.error(string); // We're in a browser. Newlines are mandated
   396	  }
   397	}
   398	
   399	export function print_debug(string) {
   400	  if (typeof process === "object" && process.stderr?.write) {
   401	    process.stderr.write(string + "\n"); // If we're in Node.js, use `stderr`
   402	  } else if (typeof Deno === "object") {
   403	    Deno.stderr.writeSync(new TextEncoder().encode(string + "\n")); // If we're in Deno, use `stderr`
   404	  } else {
   405	    console.log(string); // Otherwise, use `console.log` (so that it doesn't look like an error)
   406	  }
   407	}

   425	export function power(base, exponent) {
   426	  // It is checked in Gleam that:
   427	  // - The base is non-negative and that the exponent is not fractional.
   428	  // - The base is non-zero and the exponent is non-negative (otherwise
   429	  //   the result will essentially be division by zero).
   430	  // It can thus be assumed that valid input is passed to the Math.pow
   431	  // function and a NaN or Infinity value will not be produced.
   432	  return Math.pow(base, exponent);
   433	}
   434	
   435	export function random_uniform() {
   436	  const random_uniform_result = Math.random();
   437	  // With round-to-nearest-even behavior, the ranges claimed for the functions below
   438	  // (excluding the one for Math.random() itself) aren't exact.
   439	  // If extremely large bounds are chosen (2^53 or higher),
   440	  // it's possible in extremely rare cases to calculate the usually-excluded upper bound.
   441	  // Note that as numbers in JavaScript are IEEE 754 floating point numbers
   442	  // See: <https://developer.mozilla.org/en-US/docs/Web/JavaScript/Reference/Global_Objects/Math/random>
   443	  // Because of this, we just loop 'until' we get a valid result where 0.0 <= x < 1.0:
   444	  if (random_uniform_result === 1.0) {
   445	    return random_uniform();
   446	  }
   447	  return random_uniform_result;
   448	}
   449	
   450	export function bit_array_slice(bits, position, length) {
   451	  const start = Math.min(position, position + length);
   452	  const end = Math.max(position, position + length);
   453	
   454	  if (start < 0 || end * 8 > bits.bitSize) {
   455	    return Result$Error(Nil);
   456	  }
   457	
   458	  return Result$Ok(bitArraySlice(bits, start * 8, end * 8));
   459	}
